content.text: The history of generative AI begins not with large language models but with statistical language models, restricted Boltzmann machines, and recurrent networks — architectures that laid the probabilistic and computational foundations modern systems still build on. Understanding these early models clarifies why the field evolved as it did: each generation inherited the strengths of its predecessor while engineering around its bottlenecks, whether that was intractable inference, vanishing gradients, or insufficient compute.
The first practical generative text models were n-gram models: the probability of the next word is conditioned only on the previous n−1 words. For a trigram (n=3):

attrs.latex: P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-2}, w_{t-1})

content.text: Probabilities are estimated by counting co-occurrences in a corpus and smoothing zero-count n-grams (Kneser-Ney, Good-Turing). N-gram models dominated ASR and machine translation through the 2000s. Their key limitation is the Markov assumption: they cannot capture dependencies longer than n−1 tokens. A trigram model with n=3 has no memory beyond 2 words — "The bank of the river" and "The bank of England" are indistinguishable until word 5.

from collections import defaultdict, Counter
import random


class NgramLM:
    """Simple n-gram language model with Laplace smoothing."""

    def __init__(self, n: int = 3):
        self.n = n
        self.counts: dict = defaultdict(Counter)  # context -> {next_word: count}
        self.vocab: set = set()

    def train(self, tokens: list[str]) -> None:
        for i in range(len(tokens) - self.n + 1):
            context = tuple(tokens[i : i + self.n - 1])
            next_word = tokens[i + self.n - 1]
            self.counts[context][next_word] += 1
            self.vocab.add(next_word)

    def prob(self, context: tuple[str, ...], word: str) -> float:
        """P(word | context) with Laplace smoothing."""
        V = len(self.vocab)
        total = sum(self.counts[context].values()) + V
        return (self.counts[context][word] + 1) / total

    def generate(self, seed: list[str], n_tokens: int = 20) -> list[str]:
        """Generate tokens autoregressively from a seed context."""
        tokens = list(seed)
        for _ in range(n_tokens):
            context = tuple(tokens[-(self.n - 1):])
            candidates = list(self.vocab)
            probs = [self.prob(context, w) for w in candidates]
            total = sum(probs)
            probs = [p / total for p in probs]
            tokens.append(random.choices(candidates, weights=probs)[0])
        return tokens

Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) are two-layer undirected energy-based models with visible units v (observed data) and hidden units h (latent features), with no within-layer connections. The energy is:

attrs.latex: E(v, h) = -b^\top v - c^\top h - v^\top W h

content.text: Hinton and Salakhutdinov (2006) showed that stacking RBMs layer-by-layer (Deep Belief Networks, DBNs) and pre-training greedily could initialise deep networks — a key breakthrough when random initialisation caused vanishing gradients. RBMs and DBNs were the dominant generative models for images and collaborative filtering (Netflix Prize) until GANs and VAEs arrived in 2013–2014.
RNNs break the Markov limitation by maintaining a hidden state h_t that summarises all past tokens. At each step:

attrs.latex: h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b), \qquad P(w_{t+1}) = \text{softmax}(W_o h_t)

content.text: Bengio et al. (2003) introduced the first neural language model with learned word embeddings. Mikolov et al. (2010) scaled RNN language models to large corpora and showed they outperform n-grams at perplexity. The key failure mode: vanishing gradients prevent the hidden state from propagating information more than ~10–20 tokens back.
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM, Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) adds a cell state c_t gated by input, forget, and output gates — allowing gradients to flow unchanged through the cell for many steps:

attrs.latex: \begin{aligned} f_t &= \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \\ i_t &= \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \\ c_t &= f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tanh(W_c [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c) \\ o_t &= \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \\ h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(c_t) \end{aligned}

content.text: GRU (Cho et al., 2014) simplifies the LSTM to two gates (reset and update), achieving similar performance with fewer parameters. LSTMs became the workhorse of NLP: machine translation (seq2seq with attention, Bahdanau 2015), speech recognition, text generation, and early chatbots all relied on LSTMs through 2017.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class LSTMLanguageModel(nn.Module):
    """
    Character-level LSTM language model.
    Trained to predict the next character given all previous characters.
    A simple version of the model that powered early neural text generation.
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, embed_dim: int = 64,
                 hidden_dim: int = 256, n_layers: int = 2):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.lstm  = nn.LSTM(embed_dim, hidden_dim, n_layers,
                             batch_first=True, dropout=0.2)
        self.head  = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, x: Tensor, state=None):
        """x: (B, T) token indices. Returns (logits (B,T,V), new_state)."""
        emb = self.embed(x)               # (B, T, embed_dim)
        out, state = self.lstm(emb, state) # (B, T, hidden_dim)
        return self.head(out), state       # (B, T, vocab_size)

    @torch.no_grad()
    def generate(self, seed: Tensor, n: int, temperature: float = 1.0) -> Tensor:
        """Autoregressively generate n tokens from a seed tensor."""
        tokens = seed.unsqueeze(0)        # (1, T_seed)
        _, state = self.forward(tokens)
        last = tokens[:, -1:]             # (1, 1) — last seed token
        generated = []
        for _ in range(n):
            logits, state = self.forward(last, state)  # (1, 1, V)
            probs = (logits[:, -1] / temperature).softmax(-1)
            next_token = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)   # (1, 1)
            generated.append(next_token.item())
            last = next_token
        return torch.tensor(generated)

Sutskever et al. (2014) introduced the sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) architecture: an LSTM encoder reads the source sequence and compresses it to a fixed context vector; an LSTM decoder generates the target sequence from that vector. The bottleneck of a single context vector was relieved by Bahdanau attention (2015): at each decoder step, the decoder attends over all encoder hidden states with learned alignment weights. This became the immediate predecessor of the Transformer.
Statistical text generation; backbone of ASR and MT for 20+ years
Gated cell state solves vanishing gradients; enables long-range sequence modelling
Learned word embeddings; first neural language model that generalises
Greedy layer-wise pre-training; enabled training of deep networks
Efficient distributed word embeddings; king − man + woman ≈ queen
Three paradigms that define modern generative AI all appeared in one year
Soft alignment over encoder states; relieved the fixed-context bottleneck in seq2seq
All-attention architecture; scales to billions of parameters; supersedes RNNs

Mental model: early generative models were constrained by two bottlenecks — memory and compute. N-grams had fixed, short memory (n−1 tokens). RNNs had unbounded memory in principle but couldn't train it effectively (vanishing gradients). LSTMs solved the training problem but remained sequential (no parallelism). The Transformer solved both: self-attention gives every position direct access to every other position, and the all-matrix-multiply architecture parallelises across the entire sequence. The entire arc from n-grams to GPT is a progression of removing these two bottlenecks.

Understanding early generative models is not merely historical. Several mechanisms from the pre-transformer era continue to surface in modern systems in modified forms. RNN-based components appear in streaming audio models, time-series forecasters, and edge-deployed sequence models where the autoregressive state of a transformer is too memory-intensive. Restricted Boltzmann Machines influenced the design of modern energy-based models and diffusion models, which share the same probabilistic spirit.

More importantly, the failure modes of each generation explain the design choices of the next. The vanishing gradient problem in RNNs explains why LSTMs use gated cell states. The fixed-context bottleneck in seq2seq explains why attention was invented. The sequential computation bottleneck of LSTMs explains why the transformer abandoned recurrence entirely. Reading this lineage forward makes each design decision feel necessary rather than arbitrary.

Practically, n-gram smoothing techniques (Kneser-Ney, absolute discounting) are still used in speech recognition and rare-word handling in neural models. Word2Vec embeddings remain in production pipelines at many companies because of their tiny memory footprint and fast lookup. The 2014 seq2seq paper is still the standard reference for conditional generation frameworks.
Working with N-gram Models: Perplexity and Evaluation

The standard metric for language model quality is perplexity: the geometric mean inverse probability of the test corpus under the model. Lower perplexity indicates the model assigns higher probability to the observed sequence and is therefore a better predictor. For a trigram model on N test tokens:

attrs.latex: \text{PP}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})\right)

content.text:
import math
from collections import defaultdict, Counter

class BigramLM:
    """Bigram language model with Laplace smoothing and perplexity evaluation."""

    def __init__(self):
        self.bigram_counts: dict = defaultdict(Counter)
        self.unigram_counts: Counter = Counter()
        self.vocab: set = set()

    def train(self, tokens: list[str]) -> None:
        for i in range(len(tokens) - 1):
            w_prev, w_next = tokens[i], tokens[i + 1]
            self.bigram_counts[w_prev][w_next] += 1
            self.unigram_counts[w_prev] += 1
            self.vocab.update([w_prev, w_next])

    def log_prob(self, context: str, word: str) -> float:
        """Log P(word | context) with Laplace smoothing."""
        V = len(self.vocab)
        numerator = self.bigram_counts[context][word] + 1
        denominator = self.unigram_counts[context] + V
        return math.log(numerator / denominator)

    def perplexity(self, tokens: list[str]) -> float:
        """Compute perplexity on a held-out token sequence."""
        log_sum = 0.0
        n = 0
        for i in range(len(tokens) - 1):
            log_sum += self.log_prob(tokens[i], tokens[i + 1])
            n += 1
        return math.exp(-log_sum / n)


# Example usage
corpus = (
    "the cat sat on the mat the cat sat on the mat "
    "the dog ran in the park the cat and the dog played"
).split()

lm = BigramLM()
lm.train(corpus)

test = "the cat ran in the park".split()
print(f"Perplexity on test: {lm.perplexity(test):.2f}")

# Compare with known vs. unknown bigrams
print(f"P(cat | the): {math.exp(lm.log_prob('the', 'cat')):.4f}")   # seen bigram
print(f"P(fish | the): {math.exp(lm.log_prob('the', 'fish')):.4f}") # unseen, smoothed
Practical: Comparing N-gram and LSTM Language Models

The defining quantitative advantage of LSTMs over n-gram models is their ability to exploit long-range context. This manifests in lower perplexity on corpora with long-distance dependencies. On the Penn Treebank dataset (a standard benchmark), trigram models achieve roughly 145 perplexity; a vanilla LSTM (1 layer, 650 units) achieves around 82; a two-layer LSTM with dropout reaches roughly 68. Transformers push this below 20.
Sequential; vanishing gradient at long range

False for all tasks. LSTMs remain competitive for streaming inference, very long sequences (hours of audio), and memory-constrained devices. Their O(1) per-step cost at inference time versus transformer's O(n) KV cache makes them attractive for edge deployment.
'N-gram models are too simple to be useful.'

N-gram models with Kneser-Ney smoothing are still deployed in production for spell correction, ASR beam search rescoring, and input method editors on mobile devices. Their speed (billions of lookups per second) is unmatched by neural models.

Bahdanau attention (2015) appeared two years before the Transformer (2017). The Transformer's innovation was removing recurrence entirely and using attention as the sole mixing mechanism, combined with positional encoding.
Word2Vec learns distributional similarity — words that appear in similar contexts. It captures some syntactic patterns (king-man+woman≈queen) as byproducts, but does not model grammar explicitly.
'Seq2Seq requires the encoder to process the full sequence before the decoder can start.'
True for the classic architecture. Modern variants use streaming encoders and incremental decoding to reduce latency.
Best Practices When Working with Pre-Transformer Architectures
When to still reach for an LSTM:
online learning on data streams, sequences longer than 8K tokens where transformer memory is prohibitive, models that must run on CPU or microcontrollers.
Use pre-trained Word2Vec or GloVe embeddings
as a free warm start for downstream tasks when you have limited data and cannot afford large transformer pre-training.
When benchmarking transformers, always include an LSTM baseline.
In tasks with very short sequences (< 20 tokens) and clean formatting, LSTMs sometimes match transformer performance at a fraction of the training cost.
RNNs and LSTMs require gradient clipping (clip_grad_norm_) to prevent exploding gradients. Without it, a single bad batch can corrupt all learned weights.
Kneser-Ney smoothing is worth implementing correctly.
The absolute discounting variant of Kneser-Ney is the strongest n-gram baseline. NLTK's KneserNeyProbDist provides a reference implementation.
GPU-optimised cuDNN backend; supports packed sequences
Bigram and trigram models with proper smoothing
Efficient C extension; supports millions of vocabulary items
Tokenisation, vocabulary building for RNN-era models
ELMo, older GPT-1 available as reference
Industry-standard fast n-gram LM used in ASR pipelines

# Loading a pre-trained Word2Vec model with gensim
# pip install gensim

import gensim.downloader as api

# Download Google's pre-trained Word2Vec (3M words, 300 dims) — ~1.6 GB
# wv = api.load("word2vec-google-news-300")

# For a smaller model in testing:
wv = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-50")  # 400K words, 50 dims, ~70 MB

# Vector arithmetic
result = wv.most_similar(positive=["king", "woman"], negative=["man"], topn=3)
print("king - man + woman ≈", result)
# [('queen', 0.85), ('princess', 0.75), ('monarch', 0.74)]  (approx)

# Semantic similarity
print(f"dog ~ puppy: {wv.similarity('dog', 'puppy'):.3f}")   # ~0.80
print(f"dog ~ car:   {wv.similarity('dog', 'car'):.3f}")     # ~0.20
Security and Reliability of Early Architectures

Distributional embeddings inherit biases present in the training corpus. Gender, racial, and occupational stereotypes encoded in web text are reflected in cosine similarities. Audit embeddings for bias before using them in downstream models that make consequential decisions.

N-gram language models can be poisoned by injecting adversarial sequences into training corpora. A trigram model trained on poisoned data may assign high probability to attacker-controlled completions. Validate training data sources for production n-gram deployments.

Without gradient clipping, a single batch of extreme values can cause exploding gradients that corrupt all model weights. Always clip gradients (torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)) when training RNNs or LSTMs.

Seq2Seq models can generate fluent but factually incorrect output — a problem that predates transformers and large LLMs. The fixed-context bottleneck in early seq2seq models actually increases hallucination risk relative to attention-augmented models.

Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) was a watershed moment not because it was the first word embedding model (Bengio's 2003 neural LM already used them) but because it made high-quality embeddings computationally cheap enough to train on billions of words with a single machine. The key insight was that distributional similarity — words that appear in similar contexts have similar meanings — could be captured by training a shallow neural network to predict either surrounding words from a target (Skip-gram) or a target from surrounding words (CBOW).

The mathematical intuition behind Word2Vec is the distributional hypothesis: the meaning of a word is determined by its context. Two words with similar context distributions (e.g., 'king' and 'queen') end up with similar embedding vectors. Remarkably, the difference vectors encode semantic relationships:
This geometry works because the training objective forces the model to capture relational structure. The Skip-gram objective maximises the log probability of context words given the centre word:

# Training Word2Vec from scratch using gensim
# pip install gensim

from gensim.models import Word2Vec
import re


def tokenise(text: str) -> list[list[str]]:
    """Split text into sentences of lowercased words."""
    sentences = []
    for sent in text.split("."):
        words = re.findall(r"[a-z]+", sent.lower())
        if len(words) > 3:
            sentences.append(words)
    return sentences


corpus = """
The cat sat on the mat. The dog ran in the park. The cat chased the dog.
Animals like cats and dogs are popular pets. Dogs are loyal companions.
Cats are independent animals. Both cats and dogs need food and water.
Scientists study animal behaviour. Animals communicate in different ways.
"""

sentences = tokenise(corpus)
print(f"Training on {len(sentences)} sentences")

# Train Skip-gram model (sg=1); use CBOW with sg=0
model = Word2Vec(
    sentences=sentences,
    vector_size=50,     # embedding dimension
    window=3,           # context window size
    min_count=1,        # include words seen >= min_count times
    sg=1,               # 1=Skip-gram, 0=CBOW
    epochs=200,
    workers=1,
    seed=42,
)

# Most similar words
print("Words similar to 'cat':", model.wv.most_similar("cat", topn=3))
print("Words similar to 'dog':", model.wv.most_similar("dog", topn=3))

# Vector arithmetic: cat + behaviour - animals ~ ?
result = model.wv.most_similar(
    positive=["cat", "behaviour"],
    negative=["animals"],
    topn=1
)
print("cat + behaviour - animals:", result)
GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation (2014)

GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) improved on Word2Vec by directly optimising the ratio of co-occurrence probabilities rather than predicting context words. Instead of local context windows, GloVe uses the full word co-occurrence matrix computed over the entire corpus. The weighted least-squares objective is:
is the co-occurrence count of words

is a weighting function that down-weights very frequent co-occurrences to avoid them dominating the objective. GloVe embeddings are slightly more consistent across runs (no stochastic sampling) and achieve similar or slightly better analogy performance than Word2Vec on standard benchmarks like WordSim-353.

2014 was uniquely productive for generative AI. Three landmark papers appeared within months of each other, each founding a separate paradigm that persists today. Understanding each one contextualises why modern systems look the way they do.

GANs introduced the idea that a generative model could be trained adversarially without an explicit likelihood function — solving the intractable density estimation that plagued earlier models like RBMs. VAEs introduced principled latent variable modelling with a tractable variational lower bound, enabling both generation and representation learning. Seq2seq showed that a single neural architecture could handle complex structured output, displacing decades of pipeline-based NLP systems.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn


# Minimal VAE to illustrate the 2014 paradigm shift
# Prior: z ~ N(0, I)  |  Posterior: q(z|x) = N(mu, sigma^2 I)
# Encoder outputs (mu, log_var); reparameterisation allows backprop through sampling

class VAEEncoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim: int = 784, hidden: int = 256, latent: int = 20):
        super().__init__()
        self.shared = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU())
        self.mu_head = nn.Linear(hidden, latent)
        self.logvar_head = nn.Linear(hidden, latent)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        h = self.shared(x)
        mu = self.mu_head(h)
        log_var = self.logvar_head(h)
        return mu, log_var


class VAEDecoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, latent: int = 20, hidden: int = 256, output_dim: int = 784):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(latent, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, output_dim), nn.Sigmoid(),
        )

    def forward(self, z: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        return self.net(z)


def reparameterise(mu: torch.Tensor, log_var: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    # Key trick: z = mu + eps * sigma, eps ~ N(0, I)
    # Gradients flow through mu and log_var, not through the random sample
    std = (0.5 * log_var).exp()
    eps = torch.randn_like(std)
    return mu + eps * std


def vae_loss(x_hat, x, mu, log_var):
    # Reconstruction loss (binary cross-entropy for pixel data)
    recon = nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x, reduction="sum")
    # KL divergence from N(mu, sigma^2) to N(0, I)
    kl = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum()
    return recon + kl


# Quick forward pass
enc = VAEEncoder(784, 256, 20)
dec = VAEDecoder(20, 256, 784)
x = torch.rand(4, 784)
mu, log_var = enc(x)
z = reparameterise(mu, log_var)
x_hat = dec(z)
loss = vae_loss(x_hat, x, mu, log_var)
print(f"VAE loss: {loss.item():.1f}")
print(f"Latent z shape: {z.shape}")   # (4, 20)

ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models, Peters et al. 2018) was the last major contribution of the pre-transformer LSTM era. Unlike Word2Vec and GloVe, which produce a single static vector per word, ELMo produces dynamic embeddings that depend on context: the word 'bank' in 'river bank' and 'bank account' receives different representations. This contextualisation was achieved by running a deep bidirectional LSTM language model and extracting hidden states from each layer.
ELMo representations are formed as a weighted combination of all LSTM layer outputs:
are softmax-normalised task-specific weights over layers, and
is the hidden state of layer
. Adding ELMo embeddings to task-specific models improved state-of-the-art by 6–20% across 6 NLP benchmarks in 2018, including SQuAD reading comprehension and coreference resolution.
first widely-adopted contextual word representation, directly preceding BERT.
two-layer bidirectional LSTM trained as a language model on 1 billion tokens.

representations are computed left-to-right and right-to-left separately, then concatenated — true bidirectionality (attending to all positions simultaneously) had to wait for BERT's masked self-attention.
ELMo proved that pre-training large LMs on unsupervised text produces universally useful features, the core insight that scaled into GPT and BERT.
Architecture Evolution: From N-gram to Transformer
Each generation of generative model addressed a specific failure mode of its predecessor. The table below traces this progression, showing the bottleneck that motivated each innovation:
Fixed input window, no long-range memory
Still sequential; O(n) per step at inference

Reading this table, the transformer is not a revolution that appeared from nowhere — it is the culmination of three decades of systematically removing bottlenecks. Every problem identified in one generation shaped the solution in the next.
N-gram models dominated text generation for 20+ years; their Markov assumption limits context to n−1 tokens. Smoothing (Kneser-Ney) was essential for handling unseen n-grams.
RBMs and DBNs were the dominant deep generative models (2006–2013); Hinton's greedy pre-training breakthrough enabled training of deep networks before BatchNorm and residual connections existed.
LSTMs solved the vanishing gradient problem via gated cell state; they powered seq2seq machine translation, ASR, and text generation from ~2010 to 2017.
2014 was the generative AI's pivotal year: seq2seq, GANs, and VAEs all appeared, defining three paradigms (conditional generation, adversarial synthesis, latent variable models) that persist today.
Bahdanau attention (2015) broke the fixed-context bottleneck of seq2seq and was the direct predecessor of the Transformer's self-attention mechanism.
